title: Distance to Nearest 0

content.problemStatement: Given is a `N x M` binary matrix, for each cell find its distance from the nearest `0`.
constraints: Each matrix element is either 0 or 1
At least one 0 exists in each test case
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and M — the number of rows and columns of the binary matrix. The next N lines each contain M space‑separated integers (0 or 1) describing the matrix.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines, each containing M space‑separated integers. The integer at position (i, j) is the minimum Manhattan distance from cell (i, j) to any cell containing 0.

solution.approach: The distance from every cell to the nearest zero can be obtained by a multi‑source BFS. All cells containing 0 are inserted into the queue initially with distance 0. Then we repeatedly pop a cell, explore its four neighbours and, if a neighbour has not been visited yet, set its distance to current distance + 1 and push it into the queue. Because BFS expands layer by layer, the first time we reach a cell we have found the shortest Manhattan distance to any zero. This runs in O(N·M) time per test case and uses O(N·M) extra memory for the distance array and the queue.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * M) per test case, O(total cells) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M) for the distance matrix and the BFS queue.
